questions.explanation: Headers typically contain declarations (interfaces) that multiple .cpp files include; compilation produces object code, and linking is separate.

options.A: To store function/class declarations and interfaces shared across translation units
options.B: To store compiled machine code for the target MCU
options.C: To automatically allocate RAM for global variables at runtime
options.D: To replace the linker by merging object files

questions.question: In C++, what is the primary purpose of a header file (.h/.hpp) in an embedded project?

questions.explanation: Git is a version control system used to track changes, manage branches, and collaborate on source code.
questions.question: Which tool is primarily used for software configuration management (version control) in embedded software teams?

questions.explanation: Release builds generally enable compiler optimizations and omit many debug-only checks/symbols compared to Debug.
questions.question: In Microsoft Visual C++, which build configuration is typically used to enable optimizations and disable most debug checks for deployment?

questions.explanation: The built-in module venv is invoked via 'python -m venv <dir>' to create an isolated environment.
questions.question: In Python, which statement correctly creates a virtual environment using the standard library?

questions.explanation: Keeping interfaces in headers and implementations in source files (and using forward declarations) limits recompilation and reduces dependencies.

options.A: Expose only necessary declarations in headers and hide implementation details in .cpp files (use forward declarations where possible)
options.B: Put all library code into a single header and include it everywhere
options.C: Copy/paste the library source into every module that needs it
options.D: Disable compiler warnings to avoid integration issues

questions.question: You are using an external C/C++ library in an embedded firmware project. Which approach best reduces compile time and coupling while keeping the library usable across modules?

questions.explanation: Direct Form II Transposed is popular because it uses minimal delay elements (state) and is often more numerically robust in practice.

options.A: It typically uses fewer state variables and has good numerical properties for fixed-point/limited precision
options.C: It eliminates the need for coefficient quantization
options.D: It always requires fewer multiplications than an FIR filter of any length

questions.question: In DSP for embedded audio, why is a biquad IIR filter often implemented in Direct Form II Transposed?

questions.explanation: For symmetric positive definite matrices, Cholesky is efficient and numerically stable compared to generic elimination or explicit inversion.
questions.question: You need to solve Ax = b on an embedded target where A is symmetric positive definite. Which method is typically the best choice for numerical stability and efficiency?

questions.explanation: Audio callbacks must be real-time safe; allocations and locks can block and cause dropouts/clicks. Pre-allocation and RT-safe design prevent spikes.

options.A: Pre-allocate all needed buffers and avoid malloc/new, locks, and file I/O on the audio thread
options.B: Increase the GUI refresh rate to reduce jitter
options.C: Move DSP code to the message/UI thread to keep the audio thread light
options.D: Use double-precision everywhere to reduce rounding noise

questions.question: A real-time audio plugin occasionally produces clicks. Profiling shows periodic CPU spikes caused by memory allocation in the audio callback. Which change best addresses the issue across VST3/AU/AAX?

questions.explanation: On Windows, ASIO is commonly used for low-latency pro audio; JUCE can interface with ASIO through appropriate support/driver integration.

options.A: ASIO, usually via an ASIO driver/SDK integration (or JUCE ASIO support where available)

questions.question: You are building a cross-platform audio application with JUCE and need low-latency I/O. On Windows, which API is typically preferred for professional low-latency audio, and how does JUCE commonly access it?

questions.explanation: Profiling (perf) narrows down where time is spent on real hardware; a debugger (gdb/lldb) then validates logic/state at those hotspots.

options.A: Use perf to find hot functions and call stacks, then attach gdb/lldb to inspect state and verify assumptions at the suspected code paths
options.B: Add printf statements everywhere and remove compiler optimizations
options.C: Only run static analysis on the host machine and assume the same behavior on target
options.D: Disable interrupts to make timing deterministic and then measure again

questions.question: An embedded Linux device shows a performance regression after a change. You can reproduce it only on target hardware. Which workflow best combines debugging and profiling to identify the root cause with minimal guesswork?
